exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage:
Dear Editor,

I am writing to (1) ...... a point about the recent coverage of remote working in your magazine. The article suggested that managers who allow staff to work from home are (2) ...... of touch with business realities, and that they are not truly responsible (3) ...... the results their teams achieve. Had the journalist (4) ...... their research more carefully and (5) ...... a genuine effort to speak with remote workers, they would have discovered a very different story.

Let me begin (6) ...... saying that remote workers are often more productive than their office-based colleagues. Many of them regularly apply (7) ...... internal promotions and consistently (8) ...... enormous effort into delivering high-quality results. The reality is that many companies are simply not taking (9) ...... new remote staff at the rate they once were, partly because business growth has (10) ...... down across many sectors.

I hope future articles will reflect a more balanced view of modern working arrangements.

Yours faithfully,
Marina Clarke
explanations: Fixed phrase → **make** a point about remote working.
Out of touch → **out** of touch with business realities.
Responsible **for** the results their teams achieve.
Past perfect → Had the journalist **done** their research.
Collocation → **made** a genuine effort to speak with workers.
Begin **by** saying that remote workers are productive.
Collocation → **put** enormous effort into delivering results.
Hiring phrasal verb → not taking **on** new remote staff.
Slowed **down** → business growth has slowed down.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: Sarah forwarded a confidential report to the wrong client and was formally warned for being so (RESPONSIBLE) .......
items.explanation: Negative prefix → formally warned for being **irresponsible**.

items.sentence: 'Don't worry, I can walk you through the whole process,' the trainer said, (HELP) .......
Successful (APPLY) ...... will be invited to attend a two-stage interview at our head office.
Part of my role is to (SUPERVISE) ...... the new intake of graduate trainees.
items.explanation: Verb form → to **supervise** the new intake.

items.sentence: The chief executive praised the project team for their hard work and (DEDICATE) ...... throughout the merger.
The firm announced that all (EMPLOY) ...... would be eligible for a performance-related bonus this year.
items.explanation: People employed → all **employees** eligible for a bonus.

items.sentence: The committee felt the proposed timetable was simply not (WORK) ......, so they asked the team to revise it.
All candidates must provide certified copies of their (QUALIFY) ...... before progressing to the final selection stage.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
Yuri qualified and then straight away launched his own consultancy. **than**
No ...... he launched his own consultancy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no sooner, so the correct answer is "sooner had Yuri qualified than".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "sooner had Yuri qualified than", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Women are rarely promoted to senior positions in manufacturing. **do**
Rarely ...... to senior positions in manufacturing.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inversion with rarely, so the correct answer is "do women get promoted".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "do women get promoted", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I never knew that Tony was actually the CEO of Fizzco. **realise**
Little ...... that Tony was actually the CEO of Fizzco.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is little did I, so the correct answer is "did I realise".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I only found out about the restructuring when Tracy rang. **did**
Not until ...... about the restructuring.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not until inversion, so the correct answer is "Tracy rang did I know".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "Tracy rang did I know", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I think taking on more responsibilities would benefit you. **good**
I think it will be ...... more responsibilities.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is benefit structure, so the correct answer is "good for you to get".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "good for you to get", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The two firms finally settled on the terms of the contract. **reached**
The two firms finally ...... the terms of the contract.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is settled on terms, so the correct answer is "reached an agreement on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "reached an agreement on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Staff members are never allowed to take client files off the premises. **circumstances**
Under ...... staff members allowed to take client files off the premises.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is under no circumstances, so the correct answer is "no circumstances are".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I didn't come up with a suitable response to the board's proposal until much later. **did**
Only ...... a suitable response to the board's proposal.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is only later inversion, so the correct answer is "later did I think of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "later did I think of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The data entry role was so dull that nobody was willing to take it on. **was**
Such ...... that nobody was willing to take it on.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is such inversion, so the correct answer is "a boring job was it".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "a boring job was it", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The Westfield distribution centre has been forced to close **D** down and 400 people have lost their jobs.
items.answer: D – down and 400 people have lost their jobs.
acceptedAnswers: D – down and 400 people have lost their jobs.

items.sentence: The shift leader asked Kim to stand **A** in for him while he went into town.
items.answer: A – in for him while he went into town.
acceptedAnswers: A – in for him while he went into town.

items.sentence: The logistics coordinator asked me to see **F** to the orders while he spoke to a customer.
items.answer: F – to the orders while he spoke to a customer.
acceptedAnswers: F – to the orders while he spoke to a customer.
items.explanation: Deal with orders → see **to** the orders.

items.sentence: We learned this morning that FreshFoods have taken **C** over Cookright and plan to close some branches.
items.answer: C – over Cookright and plan to close some branches.
acceptedAnswers: C – over Cookright and plan to close some branches.

items.sentence: Patricia says she never set **G** out to become a millionaire - it just happened!
items.answer: G – out to become a millionaire - it just happened!
acceptedAnswers: G – out to become a millionaire - it just happened!
items.explanation: Intend from the start → never set **out** to become a millionaire.

items.sentence: It seems that Deluxe Bakeries are bringing **B** out a new flavour of ice cream next month.
items.answer: B – out a new flavour of ice cream next month.
acceptedAnswers: B – out a new flavour of ice cream next month.
items.explanation: Launch a product → bringing **out** a new flavour.

items.sentence: After years in finance, Marco is thinking of setting **E** up a business selling his own jewellery.
items.answer: E – up a business selling my own jewellery.
acceptedAnswers: E – up a business selling my own jewellery.
items.explanation: Start a business → setting **up** a jewellery business.

items.sentenceAfter: for this managerial position is negotiable and includes a generous bonus package.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "salary" — annual pay for professionals.

wrongOptions.A: "wage" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "salary" is correct.
wrongOptions.wage: "wage" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "salary" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "payment" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "salary" is correct.
wrongOptions.payment: "payment" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "salary" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "tip" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "salary" is correct.
wrongOptions.tip: "tip" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "salary" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "salary" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: Following the company merger, nearly fifty members of staff were

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed collocation, so the correct answer is "made".

wrongOptions.A: "done" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.
wrongOptions.done: "done" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.
wrongOptions.B: "given" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.

wrongOptions.taken: "taken" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the words before and after the gap together — collocations are heard as a single chunk.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Some word pairs are fixed collocations — the correct option is the one native speakers combine naturally.
